one_line_takeaway: AttriGuard reframes IPI defense from classifying untrusted text to causally attributing why a tool call was proposed, using teacher-forced shadow replay under attenuated observations plus a fuzzy survival test, achieving 0% static ASR with near-undefended utility and single-digit ASR (6.6-9.8%) under a strong adaptive/optimization-based attacker where the best prior defenses degrade to 30-90% ASR.
key_finding: On AgentDojo static attacks, AttriGuard drives ASR to 0.00% across all four attack types on all evaluated model/suite combinations (e.g., Table 1: Gemini-2.5 average ASR 0.00% vs. 16.89% for no defense; Llama3.3-70B average ASR 0.00% vs. 15.48% for no defense), while preserving benign utility close to the undefended baseline (Gemini-2.5 average BU 67.39% vs. AttriGuard's own average UA 52.32%; the paper separately reports 'negligible' BU degradation, ~3%, in the abstract/intro, with one notable exception: Llama3.3-70B on the Travel suite shows a roughly 20% BU drop attributed to long tool-call trajectories destabilizing the shadow replay). Only CaMeL among baselines also reaches 0% ASR, but at a much larger utility cost (Gemini-2.5 average BU 38.04% for CaMeL vs. 67.39% for AttriGuard) and roughly 5x token cost vs. AttriGuard's roughly 2-3x. Runtime overhead (Table 3, Gemini/Llama): AttriGuard latency 32.40s/41.22s vs. 11.48s/14.23s for no defense (~3x), versus CaMeL at 165.47s/105.44s and IPIGuard at 102.91s/51.51s (one to two orders of magnitude higher than undefended). Under adaptive optimization-based attacks (Table 5/6), AttriGuard stays in single digits (6.56% ASR on Gemini-2.5, 9.84% on Llama3.3, unchanged from 200 to 500 query budget), while the strongest static baselines degrade sharply under the same adaptive attacker: PIGuard 52.46-57.38%, PromptArmor 29.51-31.15%, MELON 81.97-90.16% (Gemini-2.5); PIGuard 55.74%, PromptArmor 40.98%, MetaSecAlign 24.59% (Llama3.3). Ablations (Table 4, GPT-4.1-mini, ToolKnowledge attack) show each component matters: lambda=1 (weaker attenuation) raises ASR to 12.17%; replacing the fuzzy judge with strict argument matching keeps ASR at 0% but drops BU/UA to 64.71%/63.04%; removing the batching/scheduling instruction raises ASR to 0.43%; executing directly on attenuated observations (instead of using attenuation only for the shadow-verification branch) keeps 0% ASR but yields the worst utility (BU/UA 61.76%/54.35%). The paper's own failure-mode analysis of the rare successful adaptive attacks found they all exploited a single injected objective ('visit www.true-informations.com') that plausibly overlaps with a legitimate task subgoal, i.e., cases bordering the paper's own 'explicit delegation' non-goal boundary rather than defense breaks in the causal-attribution mechanism itself.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a new defense paradigm the authors call 'action-level causal attribution' for defending LLM agents against indirect prompt injection (IPI), plus a concrete runtime system, AttriGuard, that instantiates it. Instead of classifying whether untrusted tool-output text 'looks malicious' (the what-centric framing the authors attribute to prior detection/prompting/training-based defenses), AttriGuard asks why a proposed tool call was produced. At each agent step, it (1) incrementally builds a control-attenuated ('sanitized') view of past observations via HIERATTENUATE (three composable LLM-rewriting operators: structural flattening of JSON/function-call-like text, second-to-third-person perspective rewriting, and causal-scaffolding weakening); (2) performs a 'teacher-forced shadow replay' of the agent on this attenuated context, reusing the real action history verbatim to avoid trajectory-divergence confounds; and (3) applies a three-step FUZZYSURVIVE test (function-name match -> canonicalized exact-argument match -> LLM-judge adjudication) to decide whether each originally proposed tool call still 'survives' under attenuated observations. Calls that do not survive are blocked and replaced with an empty result plus an injection warning appended to history.
threat_model: Defined explicitly in Section 2.3. Attacker: cannot modify user input, system prompt, or model weights; injects arbitrary payloads only into tool-call observations, at any step. Two attacker classes are evaluated: a 'Realistic Attacker' with no knowledge of the target system/defense and no runtime visibility (uses transferable static payloads), and an 'Adaptive Attacker' with full white-box knowledge of the agent and deployed defense, observable runtime feedback, and an optimization loop (adapted from Nasr et al.'s MAP-Elites-based red-teaming framework) to craft defense-aware payloads. Defender: black-box access to the agent LLM (can intercept execution traces At/Ot and issue extra inference calls, but cannot inspect activations/attention), with an explicit carve-out that API-based fine-tuning is in-scope for comparison against training-based baselines. Two explicit non-goals are stated: (1) text-to-text attacks that never invoke tools, and (2) 'pure explicit delegation,' where the user has authorized untrusted content to fully determine a specific action/argument (e.g., 'complete the to-do list in this file').
limitations: The paper states these itself: (1) AttriGuard is explicitly 'not provably robust' -- because it does not isolate external data during planning (unlike CaMeL), it remains theoretically susceptible to optimization-based attacks. (2) It incurs moderate but non-trivial overhead: ~3x latency and up to ~2x token cost relative to an undefended agent (target-agent token cost on Llama is roughly 2x; on Gemini it is closer to parity, which the authors attribute to the undefended Gemini baseline itself being inflated by successful injection sub-trajectories). (3) Utility can degrade meaningfully on long, tool-intensive trajectories with less-stable open-weight backbones (the ~20% BU drop for Llama3.3-70B on the Travel suite, up to 18 tool calls). (4) The residual failure mode under adaptive attack is when the injected objective overlaps with a plausible task subgoal ('task-injection alignment'), in which case the action is legitimately intent-supported under the causal-attribution definition and the defense cannot and does not claim to block it -- the authors frame this as conceptually adjacent to their own explicit-non-goal boundary rather than a bug. (5) The attenuation operator library (structural flattening, perspective rewriting, causal-scaffolding weakening) is hand-designed and not proven monotonic in reducing control potency (the authors explicitly disclaim a 'formal monotonic guarantee'). (6) Requires an auxiliary LLM call for the shadow replay and (sometimes) the fuzzy judge, meaning it needs at least gray-box orchestration control over the agent pipeline (though it treats the target LLM itself as black-box).

datasets_used: AgentDojo (92 user tasks, 882 attack instances after non-goal filtering, across workspace/Slack/travel/banking suites)
Agent Security Benchmark (ASB), 400 sampled attack instances
quantitative_results: Static ASR: AttriGuard 0.00% across all 4 attack types (IgnorePrevious, Combined, ImportantMessages, ToolKnowledge) on Gemini-2.5 and Llama3.3-70B (Table 1)
Gemini-2.5 average BU: No Defense 57.61%, AttriGuard 67.39%, CaMeL 38.04%, IPIGuard 73.91%
Gemini-2.5 average ASR: No Defense 16.89%, AttriGuard 0.00%, CaMeL 0.00%, IPIGuard 3.43%
Llama3.3-70B average ASR: No Defense 15.48%, AttriGuard 0.00%
Latency (Gemini/Llama, seconds): No Defense 11.48/14.23, AttriGuard 32.40/41.22, CaMeL 165.47/105.44, IPIGuard 102.91/51.51
Adaptive ASR at 200 queries (Gemini-2.5): AttriGuard 6.56%, PIGuard 52.46%, PromptArmor 29.51%, MELON 81.97%
Adaptive ASR at 500 queries (Gemini-2.5): AttriGuard 6.56% (unchanged), PIGuard 57.38%, PromptArmor 31.15%, MELON 90.16%
Adaptive ASR (Llama3.3): AttriGuard 9.84%, PIGuard 55.74%, PromptArmor 40.98%, MetaSecAlign 24.59%

Ablation (GPT-4.1-mini, ToolKnowledge attack): default (lambda=2) BU/UA/ASR = 70.59%/66.96%/0.00%; lambda=1 gives 76.47%/69.57%/12.17%; lambda=3 gives 67.65%/67.39%/0.00%; strict survival (no fuzzy judge) gives 64.71%/63.04%/0.00%; no scheduling instruction gives 67.65%/64.35%/0.43%; execute-on-attenuated-obs-only gives 61.76%/54.35%/0.00%
Llama3-8B: AttriGuard BU 32.35% vs. No Defense 35.29%, with 0.00% ASR on ImportantMessages/ToolKnowledge, vs. StruQ (BU 17.65%) and SecAlign (BU 23.53%) which also reach 0.00% ASR but at lower BU
